coredata.dc:title: Single-component warm-white-light materials with high color-rendering index based on Eu3+, Tb3+-complexes co-doped Laponite® under mild reaction conditions
coredata.dc:creator: Deng, Yuchen
Li, Peng
Li, Huanrong
coredata.dc:description: Single-component white-light phosphors for UV-LED chip excitation have attracted increasing interests due to its good color stability and high color rendering index (CRI) values in solid-state lighting and display applications. However, most of such materials are prepared in harsh conditions, such as requiring extremely high temperature calcination (>1000 °C) and reductive atmosphere generally. To deal with this problem, we herein put forward a novel way to prepare the single-component white-light materials using Eu3+, Tb3+, and DPA (DPA = 2,6-Pyridinedicarboxylic acid) co-doped Laponite® through environmentally friendly preparation method (in aqueous solution) under mild reaction conditions (low calcination temperature 550 °C and air atmosphere), which exhibits a highly pure white light with good color quality (0.33, 0.34), moderate correlated color temperature value (CCT, 5544 K) and high color-rendering index value (CRI, 93).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Single-component white-light
Laponite®
High color rendering index
Moderate correlated color temperature value

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Emission spectra of a) (DPA)n@Lap and b) (DPA)n@Lap@550 °C excitated at 302 nm. Inset: CIE chromaticity coordinates and digital photograph of (DPA)n@Lap@550 °C.

a) Emission spectra of Eu5.5Tb4.5(DPA)n@Lap after excitation at different wavelengths. b) The relative emission intensity at 612 nm and at 544 nm (IEu/ITb) of Eu5.5Tb4.5(DPA)n@Lap excited at different wavelengths from 254 to 340 nm. Inset: Digital photographs of Eu5.5Tb4.5(DPA)n@Lap under different wavelengths UV light.

a) Emission spectra of Eu5.5Tb4.5(DPA)n@Lap@550 °C after excitation at different wavelengths from 254 to 365 nm. Inset: Digital photographs of Eu5.5Tb4.5(DPA)n@Lap@550 °C under different wavelengths UV light. b) CIE chromaticity coordinates and digital photograph of Eu5.5Tb4.5(DPA)n@Lap@550 °C after excitation at different wavelengths from 254 to 365 nm.
Emission spectra of Eu5.5Tb4.5(DPA)n@Lap@550 °C after excitation at 323 nm. Inset: The corresponding CIE chromaticity coordinates.

a) Emission spectra of Eu6.5Tb3.5(DPA)n@Lap@550 °C after excitation at different wavelengths from 254 to 365 nm. Inset: Digital photographs of Eu5.5Tb4.5(DPA)n@Lap@550 °C under different wavelengths UV light. b) Emission spectra of Eu6.5Tb3.5(DPA)n@Lap@550 °C after excitation at 323 nm. Inset: The corresponding CIE chromaticity coordinates.

head.ce:title: Single-component warm-white-light materials with high color-rendering index based on Eu3+, Tb3+-complexes co-doped Laponite® under mild reaction conditions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National-Local Joint Engineering Laboratory for Energy Conservation in Chemical Process Integration and Resources Utilization, School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Hebei University of Technology, GuangRong Dao 8, Hongqiao District, Tianjin, 300130, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National-Local Joint Engineering Laboratory for Energy Conservation in Chemical Process Integration and Resources Utilization
School of Chemical Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hebei provincial Key Lab of Green Chemical Technology and High Efficient Energy Saving School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Hebei University of Technology, GuangRong Dao 8, Hongqiao District, Tianjin, 300130, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Single-component white-light phosphors for UV-LED chip excitation have attracted increasing interests due to its good color stability and high color rendering index (CRI) values in solid-state lighting and display applications. However, most of such materials are prepared in harsh conditions, such as requiring extremely high temperature calcination (>1000 °C) and reductive atmosphere generally. To deal with this problem, we herein put forward a novel way to prepare the single-component white-light materials using Eu3+, Tb3+, and DPA (DPA = 2,6-Pyridinedicarboxylic acid) co-doped Laponite® through environmentally friendly preparation method (in aqueous solution) under mild reaction conditions (low calcination temperature 550 °C and air atmosphere), which exhibits a highly pure white light with good color quality (0.33, 0.34), moderate correlated color temperature value (CCT, 5544 K) and high color-rendering index value (CRI, 93).

Herein, we exploit a novel single-component white-light material with superior performance based on Eu3+, Tb3+, and DPA co-doped Laponite. The Eu3+, Tb3+-DPA complexes and Laponite hybrid material (DPA)n@Lap@550 °C) are used as red, green and blue emitters. By combing and tuning the contents of these red, green, and blue-white emitters, a single-component warm-white-light material with nearly ideal CIE coordinates (0.33, 0.34) and high CRI (93), and moderate CCT values (5544 K) upon excitation with UV light.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Solid-state white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) have the advantages of high efficiency, low energy, long lifetime and environmental friendliness, which have aroused a number of attentions for practical applications in solid-state lighting and display [1–5]. Nowadays, the most widespread approach for obtained WLEDs is to integrate blue LED chip with yellow phosphor YAG:Ce3+ (Y3Al5O12:Ce3+) [6,7]. Nevertheless, due to insufficient red-emitting composition in the YAG:Ce3+ emission, the device suffers from a low color-rendering index and high correlated color temperature, which greatly limits its wider application. The aforementioned fact severely restricts its application on indoor lighting. An effective method to overcome this shortcoming is employing near ultraviolet (NUV) chip coupled with tricolor phosphors [8–12]. Unfortunately, there exists strong reabsorption of the blue light by the red and green phosphors simultaneously in this system bringing about the different degradation rates of the individual phosphors, which can induce in color imbalance and low efficiency [13,14]. An ideal alternative strategy is to develop single-component white-light phosphors for UV-LED chip excitation [15–24], which, to date, have acquired important research progress. The main achievement methods are as follows: a single ion (Ce2+, Eu2+ or Dy3+), ion pairs with energy transfer mechanism (Ce3+- Eu2+; Ce3+- Tb3+; Eu2+- Mn2+ etc) or multiple ions with red, green and blue emission (such as Tm3+/Tb3+/Eu3+; Yb3+/Er3+/Tm3+ etc) doping silicates [25,26], phosphates [18,27], borates [28], aluminates [29] luminescence materials. Nonetheless, most of such materials are generated requiring harsh conditions such as reductive atmosphere and high temperature calcination (>1000 °C) generally. Therefore, it is urgently necessary to search high quality single-phase white-light phosphors under mild reaction conditions.

Laponite® has received extensive attention over the past two decades benefiting from its flexibly adjusted interlayer space and ultrahigh surface areas [30,31]. These excellent performances endow Laponite® with special utilizations as host matrices, for instance, ion exchange, material storage and delivery, separation, sensing [32–37] and so on. Additionally, Laponite® possess another outstanding advantage that is the swelling characteristics and transparency in aqueous solution, which provide an environmentally friendly preparation method for host-guest hybrid materials. Recently, the rapid development of the host-guest chemistry with Laponite® provides a new prospective for advanced multifunctional materials. So far, numerous Laponite® host-guest luminescent materials such as flexible, transparent luminescent self-standing film [38,39], luminescent supramolecular hydrogels [40], reversible phase hydrogel [41], have been reported. However, using Laponite® as host matrices to obtain single-phase white-light materials has been rarely reported. Herein we exploit a single-component white-light materials based on Eu3+, Tb3+, and DPA co-doped Laponite® (abbreviated as EuxTby(DPA)n@Lap@550 °C) through environmentally friendly preparation method (in aqueous solution) under mild reaction conditions (low calcination temperature 550 °C and air atmosphere), which exhibits a very pure white light with good color quality, moderate correlated color temperature value(CCT) and high color-rendering index value (CRI).
Laponite® was bought from Rockwood Additives Ltd. 2,6-Pyridinedicarboxylic acid (DPA), sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3), EuCl3·6H2O and TbCl3·6H2O were purchased from J&K Scientific Ltd.

Laponite® (1.0 g) was put into 10 mL double distilled water under sonication dispersion method to get colloidal solution. After centrifugation, it was washed three times with ultrapure water to obtain a wet gel. Subsequently, 0.1 mmol of DPA was dissolved in double distilled water with a moderate amount of NaHCO3 to adjust the pH to 7–8. Then (DPA)n@Lap was obtained through centrifugation, washing and drying processes.
The as-prepared (DPA)n@Lap was put in a muffle furnace and annealed in air under 550 °C for 2 h to get the last product (named as (DPA)n@Lap@550 °C).

Laponite® (1.0 g) was put in 10 mL double distilled water under sonication dispersion method to get colloidal solution. An ethanol solution, x mL TbCl3·6H2O (0.1 M) and y mL EuCl3·6H2O (0.1 M) (x + y = 10) were added to the above-mentioned suspension solution, which then were stirred at 60 °C for 24 h. After centrifugation, it was washed three times with ultrapure water to obtain a wet gel. Subsequently, 0.1 mmol of DPA was dissolved in double distilled water with a moderate amount of NaHCO3 to adjust the pH to 7–8. When the above solution became clear, the aforementioned wet gel was added and sonicated for 1 h, then EuxTby(DPA)n@Lap was obtained through centrifugation, washing and drying processes.
The as-prepared EuxTby(DPA)n@Lap was put in a muffle furnace and annealed in air under 550 °C for 2 h to get the last product (named as EuxTby(DPA)n@Lap@550 °C).

The steady-state luminescence spectra were tested on an Edinburgh Instruments FS920P using a 450 W xenon lamp. The UV–vis spectra were performed on a Agilent cary100 spectrometer. X-ray fluorescence spectroscopy were obtained by MagixPW2403. Elemental analysis was carried out on Flash EA 1112. The powder XRD data was acquired on a Bruker D8 diffractometer, using Cu-Kα radiation. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) observations were performed on a Tecnai G2 F20 microscope. Samples for thermogravimetric (TGA) studies were transferred to open platinum crucibles and analyzed by using a SDT-TGQ 600 at a heating rate of 10 °C/min using dried air as purging gas.

We choose Laponite® hybrid materials containing Eu3+ and Tb3+-DPA as red-light-emitting and green-light-emitting component for single-component white-light materials, because of their sharp and intense characteristic emission [42,43]. As far as we known, the introduction of blue emission, red emission and green emission in a single material is the prerequisite for adjusting the emission color under ultraviolet excitation. Therefore, entering or producing of blue-light-emitting component within Laponite® is vital for generating white light emission. To our surprise, a novel blue-white-emitting Laponite® hybrid material ((DPA)n@Lap@550 °C) was obtained through the intercalation of DPA within the interlayer of Laponite® in aqueous solution and roasting at 550 °C under air atmosphere. The successful intercalation of DPA into the interlayer space of Laponite® caused the decrease of 2θ (2θ = 7.1° for the pristine Laponite®, 2θ = 6.8° for (DPA)n@Lap), illustrating the expansion of interlayer spaces (d spacing) of Laponite® according to the Bragg equation (2dsinθ = nλ) (the d spacing for the pristine Laponite® is 12.4 Å, and that for (DPA)n@Lap) is 13.0 Å) [44], as shown in X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern (Fig. S1). The aforementioned fact confirm the formation of (DPA)n@Lap). The DPA loadings were decided to be approximately 0.6/u.c by elemental analysis. As shown in Fig. S2, the TGA curve of (DPA)n@Lap shows that the weight loss process is very slow from 120 to 500 °C, and the weight loss is severe when the temperature rises to 550 °C. The formation of CDs confined in the Laponite® interlayer occured from 120 to 550 °C. In addition, TEM image of (DPA)n@Lap@550 °C shows several black dots on the Laponite® platelets (Fig. S3), confirming the situ formation of CDs within the interlayer spaces of Laponite®. (DPA)n@Lap nanocomposites display no obvious luminescence colors under UV light illumination (Fig. S4). The emission spectra of (DPA)n@Lap exhibits two weak emission band at 430 nm and 468 nm for different electronic transition processes [45] under 270–365 nm UV light excitation (black line in Fig. 1 ), which can be proved to be fluorescence according to the lifetime with nanosecond scale (Fig. S5). Notably, after calcining at 550 °C, the emission intensities have an obvious increase (Fig. 1). The resulting material ((DPA)n@Lap@550 °C) reveals bright blue-white light under UV light (red line and inset in Fig. 1). The corresponding CIE value is analyzed to be (0.20, 0.22) (The inset in Fig. 1). The aforementioned results may are induced by the n→π* transition of C=O, supported by a new absorption band from 300 to 400 nm ascribed to the n→π* transition of C=O appears (Fig. S6) [46–48].

Encouragingly, we achieve warm-white-light emission through balancing the contents of the above red, green, and blue-white emitters. As reported previously [39], Eu5.5Tb4.5(DPA)n@Lap nanocomposites are synthesized through situ formation method in aqueous solution. And the molar ratios of Eu3+/Tb3+ in Eu5.5Tb4.5(DPA)n@Lap are maybe corroborated by X-ray fluorescence spectrometry. They show tunable luminescence colors ranging from yellow to green to very weak light excited at different wavelengths UV light (the inset in Fig. 2 a). Their corresponding emission spectra (Fig. 2a and Fig. S7) mainly exhibit simultaneously characteristic emission peaks at 490, 544, 584, and 620 nm which belong to Tb3+ due to 5D4 level to the 7FJ levels (J = 6, 5, 4, 3) and strong emission lines of Eu3+ at 579, 593, 612, 650, and 698 nm from 5D0→7FJ (J = 0–4) transitions, further indicating relatively good energy transfer between DPA ligand with Eu3+ and Tb3+ ions [49]. Additionally, the emission intensity ratio of Eu3+ and Tb3+ can vary with the excitation wavelength changing from 254 to 340 nm (Fig. 2b), which match well with the above tunable luminescence colors.

To enhance the quality of white light, it is essential to increase the proportion of red component [50]. Therefore, we increase the content of Eu3+ and prepare Eu6.5Tb3.5(DPA)n@Lap@550 °C through identical way with Eu5.5Tb4.5(DPA)n@Lap@550 °C. Fig. 5 a displays the emission spectra of Eu6.5Tb3.5(DPA)n@Lap@550 °C. We observed that the emission spectrum under excitation of 323 nm provide a white-light emission with CIE value of (0.33, 0.34) (Fig. 5b), approaching those of the pure white light (0.33, 0.33). The CRI value is so high as 93 and CCT value reduce to 5544 K, which meet well with the light source of an ideal warm-white-light-emitting system [51,52]. Under the excitation wavelength of 305 nm, the CIE, CRI and CCT values became (0.35, 0.37) (Fig. S9), 94 and 4851 K, respectively.

To further explore warm-white-light luminescence mechanism, we take Eu6.5Tb3.5(DPA)n@Lap as example to conduct comparative analysis for their luminescence characteristics before and after calcinating. As shown in Fig. S10, the compared emission spectra display that strong emission band at 430 nm and 468 nm are appear, and the typical emission peaks of Eu3+ and Tb3+ have an obvious enhancement after calcinating at 550 °C. The aforementioned result caused the production of white light directly. The appearance of strong emission band at 430 nm and 468 nm are attributed to the n→π* transition of C=O [46–48]. To interpret the enhancement origin of the typical emission peaks of Eu3+ and Tb3+, we research the excitation spectra of Eu6.5Tb3.5(DPA)n@Lap before and after calcinating at 550 °C. The excitation spectra contain two strong broad bands at 284 nm and 314 nm in the region of 240 nm to 370 nm resulting from π→π* transitions of DPA and a chain of weak peaks at 355, 370 and 380 nm assigned to 7F5→5L9, 7F5→5L10, 7F5→5G6 transition of Tb3+ when observed at 5D4→7F5 transition of Tb3+ (the black line in Fig. S11) [53,54]. While when monitored at 5D0→7F2 transition of Eu3+, the excitation spectra show a strong broad band centered at 284 nm and a round of weaker sharp lines due to transitions between 7F0 and 5H6, 5D4, 5G2, 5L6, 5D3, and 5D2 levels at 318, 362, 377, 395 and 465 nm respectively (the black line in Fig. S12) [55,56]. This fact indicate the relatively good energy transfer from DPA ligand to Eu3+ and Tb3+ ions [49]. After calcinating at 550 °C, the excitation spectra of the resulting material (Eu6.5Tb3.5(DPA)n@Lap@550 °C) have undergone significant changes. The absorption peaks red-shift to 323 nm when observed at 5D4→7F5 transition of Tb3+ and 5D0→7F2 transition of Eu3+ (the red line in Fig. S11 and Fig. S12). Additionally, a chain of weak sharp peaks originated from 7F5→5L9, 7F5→5L10, 7F5→5G6 transition of Tb3+ and 7F0 to 5H6, 5D4, 5G2, 5L6, 5D3 and 5D2 levels respectively have a clear enhancement. The above results reveal that calcinating process brings about the decline of coordination effect between DPA with Tb3+ and Eu3+. Reduced carboxyl content occurs during dehydration and carbonization process brings about the decline of coordination effect between DPA with Tb3+ and Eu3+. Therefore, normally, the emission intensities of Eu3+ and Tb3+ ions should be decrease in some extent. However, conversely, the emission intensities of Tb3+ and Eu3+ (λex = 323 nm) have an obvious enhancement, this is possibly because that calcining process can remove of water molecules in the first coordination sphere of Eu3+ and Tb3+ ions partially, thereby weakening their causing luminescence quenching [57,58]. The above-mentioned are generation mechanism of white light emission.

In conclusion, we exploit a novel single-component warm-white-light material with superior performance on the basis of Eu3+, Tb3+, and DPA co-doped Laponite® through environmentally friendly preparation method (in aqueous solution) under mild reaction conditions (low calcination temperature 550 °C and air atmosphere). Herein, the Eu3+, Tb3+-DPA complexes and Laponite® hybrid material (DPA)n@Lap@550 °C are used as red, green and blue emitters. By combing and tuning the contents of these red, green, and blue-white emitters, a single-component warm-white-light material with nearly ideal CIE coordinates (0.33, 0.34) and high CRI (93), and moderate CCT values (5544 K) upon excitation with UV light, which indicate the promising application on the warm-white LED.
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21171046, 21502039, 21271060), the Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province (No. B2016202147, B2016202149, B2017202048), Educational Committee of Hebei Province (LJRC021, QN2015172), Hebei Provincial College of Science and Technology Research Project (BJ2018054), Tianjin Natural Science Foundation (18JCYBJC17200).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.optmat.2019.04.068.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Pust, P. J. Schmidt, W. Schnick, A revolution in lighting, Nat. Mater. 14 (2015) 454-458.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly efficient non-rare-earth red emitting phosphor for warm white light-emitting diodes

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ce3+, Tb3+-doped lutecium-involved silicate phosphors: synthesis, structure refinement and photoluminescence properties

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Li, S. Liang, H. Lian, M. Shang, B. Xing, J. Lin, Ce3+, Tb3+-doped lutecium-involved silicate phosphors: synthesis, structure refinement and photoluminescence properties, J. Mater. Chem. C 4 (2016) 3443-3453.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Colour tuning via crystalline site-selected energy transfer in a Sr2SiO4:Eu2+,Pr3+ phosphor

sb:title.sb:maintitle: How to produce white light in a single-phase host?

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Shang, C. Li, J. Lin, How to produce white light in a single-phase host? Chem. Soc. Rev. 43 (2014) 1372-1386.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. Sreeja, V. Vidyadharan, S. K. Jose, A. George, C. Joseph, N. V. Unnikrishnan, P. R. Biju, A single-phase white light emitting Pr3+ doped Ba2CaWO6 phosphor: synthesis, photoluminescence and optical properties, Opt. Mater. 78 (2018) 52-62.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simultaneous multi-wavelength ultraviolet excited single-phase white light emitting phosphor Ba1-x(Zr,Ti)Si3O9:xEu
